barcelona.meta_title: Barcelona Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
barcelona.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Barcelona trip from India. Euros, cards, transport, costs, pickpocket scams, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
barcelona.h1: Barcelona Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Euro and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Euro trades at a meaningful premium to the Indian rupee, with the running rate landing in the 90 to 100 INR per Euro range through 2025 and into 2026. Mental maths is reasonably easy: multiply Euros by roughly 100 to get a rough INR equivalent. Spain is at the more affordable end of Western Europe — noticeably less expensive than France or Germany at every tier.
Barcelona is also markedly cheaper than Madrid for hotels in the famous parts of town, and the food economics are excellent. A proper menu del día — a three-course set lunch that working Spaniards eat — costs less than a sit-down dinner at most Indian metro restaurants. Tapas and pintxos hopping in the Gothic Quarter or Born is one of the better food-value experiences in Europe.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Euro to Carry
sections.body:
Barcelona is broadly card-friendly. Cards work at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains like Mercadona and Carrefour, museums, and tourist sites. Contactless tap-to-pay is widely supported. Cash matters at smaller tapas bars (especially the older traditional ones in the Gothic Quarter and El Born), some Boqueria market stalls, taxis paid outside the apps, and small tipping rounds at bars where you would slip the bartender a Euro or two.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of Euros in cash from India for arrival incidentals — the Aerobus or train into the city, a cafe con leche, an early tapa or two. Withdraw additional cash on the ground only if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with Euros handles 90 percent of spending and a moderate cash cushion handles the rest.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Spain. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Euros Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest Euros you will buy are the Euros you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Barcelona, the rates at airport exchange counters and tourist-area money changers along La Rambla and around Plaça Catalunya are mediocre to poor. Spanish bank branches like CaixaBank, Santander, and BBVA offer much better rates for cash exchange, but with cards working almost everywhere the need for cash exchange is limited. Skip airport exchange counters at El Prat for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach: order Euros for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and rely on the card for almost all spending.

sections.title: Cards in Barcelona
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains, museums, and tourist sites in Barcelona. American Express works at international hotels and large stores, less reliably at smaller tapas bars. JCB has limited acceptance. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most modern card terminals. Bizum, the Spanish bank-to-bank QR system, is everywhere on signs but tied to local Spanish bank accounts and not directly usable by visitors. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Spain trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly Renfe rail tickets and timed entry to the Sagrada Família, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores like El Corte Inglés, and tourist-area restaurants, the card machine asks whether to charge in Euros or in your home currency. Always pick Euros. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Barcelona
sections.body:
Bank-branded ATMs from CaixaBank, Santander, BBVA, and Banco Sabadell are everywhere. Foreign cards work reliably and rates are reasonable. Withdrawal fees are moderate. Avoid the independent Euronet ATMs that appear in tourist-heavy areas — La Rambla, the Gothic Quarter, around Sagrada Família — their rates and fees are dramatically worse than Spanish bank ATMs.
Skip the airport ATMs at El Prat for anything beyond emergency cash.

sections.title: The Hola Barcelona Travel Card
sections.body:
Barcelona's public transport network — metro, buses, trams, and FGC suburban trains — is run on an integrated ticket system. The Hola Barcelona Travel Card gives unlimited rides for 48, 72, 96, or 120 hours and includes the airport metro line, which is otherwise an extra-fee zone. For most Indian travellers on a 4 to 7 day Barcelona trip, the Hola card pays for itself comfortably.
There is also the T-casual ticket — a 10-trip carnet that does not include the airport — which works well for shorter visits. Buy at any metro station ticket machine or through the TMB app. Tap the card on entry; on the metro and trams, no exit tap is needed.
From El Prat Airport, the Aerobus direct to Plaça Catalunya is the easiest option for first-time visitors. The L9 metro line is cheaper if you are using a Hola card. RENFE Rodalies trains link the airport to Passeig de Gràcia and Sants stations and are also a fine option.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in Barcelona splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, menu del día lunches, supermarket dinners or cheap tapas, public transport on a Hola card, free attractions like the beaches at Barceloneta, Park Guell's outdoor sections, the Gothic Quarter walks, and free-day rotations at major museums. Barcelona at this tier is among the best-value major European city breaks.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Eixample, the Gothic Quarter, or El Born, two restaurant meals a day with a mix of menu del día lunches and proper tapas dinners, public transport, paid attractions like the Sagrada Família, Park Güell with reservations, the Picasso Museum, and the Joan Miró Foundation, a flamenco show or a Camp Nou tour.
Premium: five-star hotels like the Mandarin Oriental, the Hotel Arts, or the W, fine-dining restaurants like Disfrutar, Cinc Sentits, or Tickets, private guides for the Gaudí circuit, helicopter tours, and the high-end shopping budget on Passeig de Gràcia. Barcelona at this tier is comparable to but slightly cheaper than equivalent Paris or Milan.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in Barcelona costs less than the same trip in Paris, London, or Zurich. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

indonesia.meta_title: Indonesia Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
indonesia.meta_desc: A practical guide to an Indonesia trip from India. Rupiah, USD, cards, Bali tourist tax, transport, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
indonesia.h1: Indonesia Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Rupiah and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Indonesian rupiah runs in big numbers, and the mental maths takes a day or two to settle. A nasi goreng costs around 50,000 rupiah. A villa night in Bali runs into the millions. The trick is dropping the last three zeros — divide by a thousand and the figure becomes manageable.
There is a small but important constraint Indian travellers should know. Indonesian law restricts the export of large amounts of rupiah out of the country. The practical rule for a tourist is to convert what you need on arrival, spend it, and avoid leaving with a wallet full of rupiah. Carry small leftover amounts back if you must, but do not stockpile rupiah in India before flying. The cleanest approach is to carry US dollars from India and convert on the ground, or load a forex card with USD.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Cash to Carry
sections.body:
Indonesia is mainly a cash economy for everyday spending. Warungs (small local restaurants), street food, beach bars, fruit sellers, traditional markets, intercity transport, taxis outside Jakarta and Bali tourist zones, and most local experiences run on cash. Cards work at four-star and five-star hotels, branded restaurants in Seminyak, Canggu, and Ubud, supermarket chains, and major tourist sites like the Tanah Lot temple complex.
There is one important payment-side distinction in Bali. Many warungs, cafes, and small businesses accept Indonesian QR payments through QRIS, but QRIS is tied to local bank accounts and is not directly usable by Indian travellers. The signs are everywhere; the wallet does not work for visitors.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry US dollars from India in clean, undamaged notes — torn, marked, or pre-2009 series notes are routinely refused at Indonesian exchange counters. Convert in batches on arrival rather than all at once. Keep a forex card loaded with USD as backup, both for ATM withdrawals in Indonesia and for hotel and restaurant payments where cards are accepted.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Indonesia. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Exchange Money
sections.body:
The cheapest forex you will buy is the USD you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Indonesia, the authorised money changers operating under PVA Bali licences offer the best rates — far better than banks and far better than airport counters or hotel front desks. The catch is that there are also a number of well-known scam money changers in Kuta, Seminyak, and Legian. Stick to PT Central Kuta, BMC, and other PVA-licensed brands. Verify the licence sticker on the door, count the money carefully before leaving the counter, and never accept a rate that is suspiciously better than nearby competitors.
Skip the airport exchange counters at Denpasar and Jakarta for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.

sections.title: Cards in Indonesia
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at four-star and five-star hotels, branded restaurants in Bali's tourist zones, supermarket chains like Pepito and Bintang, big shopping malls in Jakarta, and most established tour operators. Acceptance drops sharply outside those circles. American Express works at international hotel chains but not much else. JCB has decent acceptance through Japanese tourism volumes. RuPay has effectively no acceptance.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most card terminals that accept contactless. QRIS, the local QR system, is everywhere on signs but not directly usable by Indian travellers.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Indonesia trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly for domestic flights and ferries, fail at the OTP step.
At hotels and large restaurants, the card machine sometimes asks whether to charge in rupiah or in your home currency. Always pick rupiah. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Indonesia
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere in Bali, Jakarta, and the major tourist towns. Bank Mandiri, BCA, BNI, and BRI are the main networks. Foreign cards work at most of them, though withdrawal limits per transaction tend to be lower than what Indian travellers are used to — often capped at around 2 to 3 million rupiah per withdrawal. Plan multiple withdrawals if you need cash for a multi-day trip.
There is a long-running ATM skimming concern in Bali. Use ATMs inside bank branches, large hotels, and major tourist centres rather than standalone roadside ATMs. Cover the keypad when entering your PIN. Check your account afterwards for unfamiliar transactions.
Skip the airport ATMs for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are high and the rates are poor.

sections.title: The Bali Tourist Tax
sections.body:
Since February 2024, Bali charges a tourist levy of IDR 150,000 per foreign tourist on arrival. The levy is paid online before flying through the official Love Bali portal, or on arrival at Denpasar Airport via card or cash. Pay before flying — the airport queues for on-arrival payment can be long. Keep the receipt, in case you are asked to show it at attractions.
The levy applies once per visit, not per night. Children are charged the same as adults. The funds go toward Bali's environmental and cultural protection programmes.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in Bali splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel or homestay, two warung meals, one cheap restaurant dinner, scooter rental, free beaches and rice paddies. Bali at this tier is among the cheapest serious-travel destinations in Asia.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a private villa or boutique hotel in Seminyak, Canggu, or Ubud, two restaurant meals a day, a private driver for day trips, paid attractions like Tanah Lot or the Sacred Monkey Forest, occasional spa visits and surf lessons.
Premium: a five-star resort in Nusa Dua or Ubud's hill country, fine-dining restaurants, private guides, helicopter tours, the high-end wellness retreat budget. Bali at this tier rivals the Maldives for honeymoon spend.
Jakarta is similar tier-for-tier but feels more business-traveller than holiday. Yogyakarta and the smaller islands run cheaper across all three tiers.
A 7-day mid-range Bali trip for two is one of the better-value beach holidays you can plan from India. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

japan.meta_title: Japan Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
japan.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Japan trip from India. Yen, cards, cash, transport, etiquette, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
japan.h1: Japan Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Yen and the Rupee
sections.body:
The yen has been weak for most of the last year. That includes against the rupee. For Indian travellers, Japan in 2026 is meaningfully cheaper than it was five years ago — hotels, dining, and shopping all feel lighter on the wallet than the Instagram reels suggest.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill.
On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands. It is the biggest avoidable cost on a Japan trip, and it gets paid before you have even left India.

sections.title: How Much Yen to Carry
sections.body:
Japan was a cash-only country for decades. It is not anymore. Contactless cards, Suica, and QR wallets work at most chains, train stations, convenience stores, hotels, and a growing share of small restaurants in Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, and Fukuoka.
Cash still matters at small ramen shops, traditional sento, some shrines and temples, rural ryokan, and older vending machines. None of those need much. A moderate stock of yen handles them all.
A Sensible Cash Plan
For a 7-to-10-day trip, carry enough yen for two days of incidentals in your wallet. Keep the rest in the hotel safe. Cards and Suica handle everything else.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Japan. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Yen Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest yen are the ones bought in India before departure. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
On a few lakh rupees of forex, the gap between a marked-up rate and the interbank rate is larger than any discount or coupon a forex provider would realistically offer. The saving lives in the rate itself, not in promotions.
The cleanest approach is to order yen for door delivery before flying, and load the balance on a forex card. The card sits in your wallet as backup currency if cash runs short.

sections.title: Cards in Japan
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted almost universally in Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, and Fukuoka. American Express works at most hotels and large stores. JCB, the Japanese network, works everywhere. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work widely, especially when paired with a Suica layer. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Japan trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, electronics stores like BicCamera and Yodobashi, and duty-free counters, the card machine asks whether to charge in yen or in rupees. Always pick yen. The rupee option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: The Best ATMs in Japan
sections.body:
Japan has one of the cleanest ATM situations in the world for foreign cards.
Seven Bank ATMs, in every 7-Eleven, are the default. Full English interface, Visa and Mastercard accepted, flat fee per withdrawal, open 24 hours. Lawson and Family Mart ATMs work the same way. Japan Post Bank ATMs are everywhere and charge a comparable fee.
Skip the currency exchange kiosks in Shibuya, Shinjuku, and Dotonbori. They run several percentage points off the mid-market rate. Hotel front desks run worse.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, two conbini meals, one cheap restaurant meal, local trains, free temples. The cheapest sensible way to do Japan.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-star business hotel like APA, Toyoko Inn, or Sotetsu Fresa, set-meal restaurants, occasional shinkansen, paid temple entries, one or two attractions a day.
Premium: four-or-five-star hotel, kaiseki dinners, taxis, JR Pass, the curated experiences. Comfortable rather than extravagant.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two lands in the lower-middle of this range on the ground. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
Trains run Japan. The system is fast, on time, and confusing on day one. Two things make it manageable.
The Suica or Pasmo IC card is the first. Tap in, tap out, fare deducts automatically. It works on almost every train, subway, bus, and convenience store across the country. The Welcome Suica Mobile app, which JR East launched in late 2024, lets you provision a virtual Suica on Apple Wallet or Google Pay before you fly. Top up with an Indian Visa or Mastercard. The airport ticket-machine queue disappears.
The JR Pass is the second, and it is no longer the obvious choice it used to be. After the October 2023 price hike, the 7-day pass is only worth it if your itinerary covers Tokyo to Kyoto plus Kyoto to Hiroshima return, or comparable long-distance shinkansen travel. For a Tokyo-Kyoto-Osaka loop, individual shinkansen tickets work out cheaper. Run the numbers on Japan Guide's calculator before you buy.
PayPay, the biggest Japanese QR wallet, does not accept foreign cards for top-up. Indian travellers cannot use it even though the signs are everywhere. Stick to Suica, contactless, and cash.

london.meta_title: London Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
london.meta_desc: A practical guide to a London trip from India. GBP, contactless, the Tube, costs, theatre tickets, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
london.h1: London Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Pound and the Rupee
sections.body:
The British pound is the most expensive major currency Indian travellers regularly use, with the running rate landing in the 125 to 132 INR per pound range through 2025 and into 2026. Mental maths is straightforward but humbling: multiply pound prices by roughly 130 to get a rough INR equivalent, and London prices reveal themselves clearly. A coffee in central London is the price of a sit-down lunch in a smaller European city. A pub lunch is the price of a proper restaurant dinner elsewhere. A West End theatre ticket in a good seat runs into several thousand rupees. Plan accordingly.
The pound has been broadly stable to slightly weaker against the rupee through the period, which is mildly helpful for Indian travellers but does not change the underlying expense level. London is comparable to Zurich at the high end and noticeably more expensive than Paris or Madrid for most categories.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Pound to Carry
sections.body:
London is among the most cashless cities in the world. Cards work at almost every retail outlet, restaurant, hotel, taxi, museum, and tourist site. Contactless tap-to-pay is universal at modern terminals and on the Transport for London (TfL) network. Cash matters at some traditional pubs that still prefer it, certain market stalls at Borough Market, Camden Market, and Brick Lane, the occasional independent cafe, and street vendors selling chestnuts or hot dogs. That is roughly the complete list.
A small stock of pounds in cash handles every situation that still needs it. Most travellers carry a modest amount and barely use it through the trip.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of pounds in cash from India for arrival incidentals — though even this is increasingly optional given how cashless London is. Withdraw additional cash on the ground only if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with pounds handles 95 percent of spending, and contactless on a phone or watch handles the rest including transport.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to the UK. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. A typical London trip can approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold once flights, hotels, and on-ground spending are added together — if your total LRS spending in the financial year crosses that threshold, the 20 percent TCS applies on the amount above ₹10 lakh, and it is recoverable against your final income tax liability when you file your return.

sections.title: Where to Buy Pounds Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest pounds you will buy are the pounds you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside London, the rates at airport exchange counters at Heathrow, Gatwick, Stansted, and Luton are notoriously poor. Bureaux de change in central London — particularly the chains in Oxford Street and around Leicester Square — vary widely. Major bank branches like Barclays, HSBC, and Lloyds offer reasonable rates but the spread is narrow against a forex card. Skip airport exchange counters for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach: order pounds for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and rely on the card and contactless for almost all spending.

sections.title: Cards in London
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted almost universally in London — at hotels, restaurants, shops, museums, theatres, and the entire TfL transport network. American Express works at most international hotels and large restaurants, less reliably at small independent cafes and pubs. JCB has reasonable acceptance at international hotels. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay, Google Pay, Samsung Pay, and contactless from a watch all work everywhere contactless is accepted, which is essentially everywhere. The default behaviour for most spending is tap-to-pay with a phone or card.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in the UK trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly West End theatre tickets and National Rail train fares, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores like Harrods and Selfridges, and most tourist-area restaurants, the card machine asks whether to charge in pounds or in your home currency. Always pick pounds. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes — and on London-sized bills, that markup can be a meaningful sum.

sections.title: ATMs in London
sections.body:
Bank-branded ATMs (called cashpoints in the UK) from Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, NatWest, and Santander are everywhere, free to use for any card, and offer reasonable rates. Independent ATMs in convenience stores, particularly those marked with fee notices on the screen, charge withdrawal fees of a few pounds per transaction. Stick to the bank-branded cashpoints.
Withdrawal limits are generous and free withdrawals from bank cashpoints make moderate one-off withdrawals economical if you do need cash. Skip the airport ATMs for anything beyond emergency cash.

sections.title: Contactless on TfL and the Daily Cap
sections.body:
The Transport for London network — the Tube, buses, the DLR, the Overground, the Elizabeth Line, trams, and most Thames Clippers river boats — runs on a single integrated payment system that accepts contactless tap-to-pay from any Visa or Mastercard, including Indian forex cards. There is no need to buy a separate Oyster card unless you specifically want one as a souvenir.
Tap your contactless card or phone on the yellow reader at the gate when entering and again when leaving (on the Tube and rail). The system tracks your journeys across the day and applies daily and weekly fare caps automatically — meaning you never pay more than the price of a one-day or seven-day Travelcard regardless of how many trips you take. The cap maths is one of the better consumer protections in any transport system in the world. Use the same card consistently across the trip to benefit from the weekly cap.
Buses are contactless-only and do not accept cash. Black taxis (the proper London cabs) accept contactless. Most station ticket machines also accept contactless if you need to top up an Oyster card or buy a paper ticket.
From Heathrow, the Elizabeth Line is the easiest and fastest option to central London, accepting contactless and capped at the same daily rate as other zones. The Heathrow Express is faster but significantly more expensive. The Piccadilly Line on the Tube is the slowest but cheapest. Avoid airport taxis from Heathrow into central London unless you have specific accessibility or luggage needs — fares run to north of £80.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in London splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, supermarket meals from Tesco or Sainsbury's, free attractions like the British Museum, the National Gallery, the Tate Modern, the Tate Britain, and Hyde Park, contactless on the bus and Tube. London at this tier is feasible but still expensive — there is no genuinely cheap London.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Bloomsbury, South Kensington, or Paddington, two restaurant meals a day with a mix of pub lunches and proper restaurant dinners, paid attractions like the Tower of London, Westminster Abbey, the Churchill War Rooms, and the London Eye, a West End theatre evening, and possibly a day trip to Windsor, Oxford, or Cambridge.
Premium: five-star hotels like the Savoy, Claridge's, the Ritz, or the Connaught, fine-dining at Restaurant Gordon Ramsay or Sketch, premium West End seats, private guides, and the high-end shopping budget on Bond Street and Sloane Street. London at this tier rivals any premium destination in the world.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in London is one of the more expensive holidays you can plan from India, comparable to Switzerland and noticeably more than Paris or Barcelona. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

malaysia.meta_title: Malaysia Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
malaysia.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Malaysia trip from India. Ringgit, cards, transport, costs, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
malaysia.h1: Malaysia Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Ringgit and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Malaysian ringgit (MYR) trades comfortably above the Indian rupee, with the running rate landing around 19 to 21 INR per MYR through 2025 and into 2026. Mental maths is easy: multiply MYR amounts by roughly 20 to get a rough INR equivalent. The ringgit has been broadly stable to slightly weak against the rupee through the period, which is helpful for Indian travellers.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.
Malaysia is genuinely affordable. Hotels, dining, and most experiences are noticeably cheaper than Singapore or Bangkok. Petaling Street, Bukit Bintang, and the hawker corners of Penang offer some of the best food value in Southeast Asia for the spend.

sections.title: How Much Ringgit to Carry
sections.body:
Malaysia sits in the middle of the cash-versus-card spectrum. Cards work at most hotels, branded restaurants, supermarket chains like Cold Storage and Jaya Grocer, big shopping malls in KL and Penang, and most established tour operators. Cash matters at hawker stalls, smaller family-run restaurants, traditional markets, intercity buses, and most local experiences in smaller towns and the East Malaysian states.
Malaysia has its own QR payment systems — DuitNow QR is the dominant one — which appear on signs everywhere. These are tied to local Malaysian bank accounts and are not directly usable by Indian travellers.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a moderate amount of MYR in cash from India for arrival incidentals — a hawker meal on day one, a taxi or train from KLIA, a bottle of water. Withdraw additional cash on the ground if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with MYR plus a moderate cash cushion handles 90 percent of spending comfortably.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Malaysia. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Ringgit Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest ringgit you will buy is the ringgit you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Malaysia, the licensed money changers at Mid Valley, KLCC, and Pavilion in KL, and at the Komtar mall and Gurney Plaza in Penang, offer rates close to the interbank rate, often better than banks. Major banks like Maybank, CIMB, and Public Bank are reliable and only slightly worse. Avoid airport exchange counters at KLIA and Penang International for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach: order a moderate amount of MYR for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and exchange in batches at licensed money changers if you need more cash on the ground.

sections.title: Cards in Malaysia
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at most hotels, branded restaurants, supermarket chains, big shopping malls, and most established tour operators in KL, Penang, Langkawi, Kota Kinabalu, and Kuching. Acceptance drops at smaller restaurants, hawker stalls, and rural areas. American Express works at most international hotels and large stores, less reliably at small shops. JCB has decent acceptance through Japanese tourism volumes. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most card terminals that accept contactless. Samsung Pay also works. DuitNow QR is everywhere on signs but tied to local bank accounts. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Malaysia trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly for AirAsia and Firefly domestic flights, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels and large department stores, the card machine asks whether to charge in MYR or in your home currency. Always pick MYR. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Malaysia
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere in KL, Penang, Langkawi, and the major cities — Maybank, CIMB, Public Bank, RHB, and Hong Leong all have wide networks. Foreign cards work reliably at most of them. Withdrawal fees are moderate. Withdrawal limits per transaction are reasonable, often higher than in Sri Lanka or Vietnam.
Skip the airport ATMs at KLIA for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are slightly higher than in-city ATMs. The licensed money changers at the airport offer better rates if you need a meaningful amount.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, hawker meals at places like Jalan Alor or Penang's Chulia Street, public transport, free attractions like the Batu Caves and Petronas Towers' outdoor area. Malaysia at this tier is one of the better value-for-money serious-travel destinations in Asia.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Bukit Bintang or KLCC, two restaurant meals a day, a private car for day trips, paid attractions like the Petronas Towers observation deck, the KL Tower, and the Aquaria, and short island hops to Langkawi or Penang. East Malaysian additions like Mount Kinabalu treks or orangutan sanctuaries sit on top.
Premium: five-star hotels like the Mandarin Oriental KL, the St Regis Langkawi, or the Datai, fine-dining restaurants, private guides, and the high-end shopping budget at Pavilion or Suria KLCC. Malaysia at this tier is comparable to Bangkok and noticeably cheaper than Singapore.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two split between KL and Langkawi or Penang is one of the better-value beach-plus-city holidays you can plan from India. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.
Two-input live converter: enter an amount in INR or MYR, see the equivalent in the other currency.
Rate displayed: the live interbank rate (mid-market), with a small label "Live interbank rate. Updated [timestamp]."
Optional secondary line: "Most banks add a 2–4% markup to this rate."
Default amount on page load: ₹50,000 → MYR equivalent.
Data source: any reliable FX API. Cache rate for 5–15 minutes.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
KL has a clean and growing rail network — the LRT, MRT, Monorail, and KLIA Express together cover most of what a tourist needs. The Touch 'n Go card handles fares across most of the system. Buy one at any major station and top up with cash or card.
Grab is the default for taxis and bike-taxis in KL, Penang, and the major cities. Pay through the app or in cash. Avoid hailing taxis off the street unless they are airport coupon taxis or branded — overcharging is common with independent street taxis, particularly outside hotels and shopping malls.
For inter-city journeys, AirAsia and Firefly link KL to Penang, Langkawi, Kota Kinabalu, and Kuching for routinely cheap fares booked in advance. The KTM intercity train is scenic but slow. Express buses are cheap and reliable for shorter routes like KL to Melaka or KL to Cameron Highlands.

prague.meta_title: Prague Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
prague.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Prague trip from India. Czech koruna, cards, transport, costs, scams, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
prague.h1: Prague Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Czech Koruna and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Czech koruna (CZK), informally called the crown, runs at roughly 3.5 to 4 INR per koruna through 2025 and into 2026. Mental maths is easy: multiply CZK amounts by roughly 4 to get a rough INR equivalent, and the running maths takes care of itself within a day.
The koruna has been broadly stable to slightly stronger against the rupee through the period. Prague is not as cheap as it was a decade ago, but it remains one of the better-value Western and Central European cities for hotels, dining, and beer. A meal at a proper Czech pub costs noticeably less than the equivalent in Vienna or Munich.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Koruna to Carry
sections.body:
Prague is broadly card-friendly. Visa and Mastercard work at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains, museums, and tourist sites. Contactless tap-to-pay is widely supported. Cash matters at smaller traditional Czech pubs (hospody), some food stalls, certain market vendors, the occasional museum, and a few transport ticket machines.
One important Prague-specific issue: the city accepts Euros at many tourist-area shops and restaurants but at distinctly poor rates. The change comes back in CZK at a rate the merchant chooses, which is usually 10 to 20 percent off the mid-market rate. This is the single most common quiet rip-off in Prague tourism. Always pay in koruna if you can.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of CZK in cash from India for arrival incidentals — train ticket from the airport (Prague Airport has no metro, only buses and trams), a coffee, a quick beer at a non-touristy pub. Withdraw additional cash on the ground if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with EUR plus a moderate cash cushion in CZK handles 95 percent of spending.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Czechia. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Koruna Before You Fly
sections.body:
This is the part of Prague trip planning where most travellers go wrong. Czech koruna is not stocked in cash by most Indian forex providers. The standard advice is to carry Euros from India, withdraw a small amount of CZK from a Prague ATM on arrival, and use a forex card with EUR loaded for most spending. A separate option is to carry a moderate amount of USD and exchange a small amount on arrival.
Inside Prague, the licensed exchange offices in the Old Town and Wenceslas Square run a very wide spread. Some advertise no commission but apply terrible rates; others advertise great rates with hidden commissions. The well-rated, reputable money changers — eXchange in particular — display "WE BUY at" and "WE SELL at" rates clearly with no commission, and they honour what is on the board. Stick to those. Avoid every other exchange office unless it is a major bank.
Skip the airport exchange counters at Vaclav Havel Airport for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.

sections.title: Cards in Prague
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains, museums, and tourist sites in Prague. American Express works at international hotel chains and large stores, less reliably at smaller pubs and shops. JCB has limited acceptance. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most modern card terminals. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Czechia trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly Czech Railways tickets and individual museum bookings, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores, and tourist-area restaurants, the card machine asks whether to charge in CZK or in your home currency. Always pick CZK. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Prague
sections.body:
ATMs in Prague come in two clear groups, and the difference between them is large enough to matter. Major bank ATMs — Ceska Sporitelna, KB, CSOB, and UniCredit — are the safe choice. They charge moderate fees and offer rates close to the interbank. The independent Euronet machines that dominate tourist areas, with bright yellow branding and Euronet logos, charge dramatically worse rates and aggressive conversion fees. Avoid them, even if they are the closest ATM at the moment.
Skip the airport ATMs for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are higher than in-city bank ATMs.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in Prague splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, two meals at non-tourist Czech pubs (where a hearty meal with a beer can cost less than ₹500), public transport on the integrated metro-tram-bus pass, free walking tours and the outdoor parts of Prague Castle. Prague at this tier is one of the better-value European city breaks.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel near the Old Town or Lesser Town, two restaurant meals a day with one at a proper Czech restaurant and one at an international or modern Prague restaurant, public transport, paid attractions like the Castle complex, the Astronomical Clock, the Klementinum, and a Vltava river cruise.
Premium: five-star hotels like the Four Seasons or the Augustine, fine-dining restaurants like Field, La Degustation, or Eska, private guides for the Castle and Jewish Quarter, a high-end Bohemian crystal shopping budget. Prague at this tier is genuinely good value compared to Western European capitals.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in Prague costs noticeably less than the same trip in Paris, London, or Vienna. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.
Two-input live converter: enter an amount in INR or CZK, see the equivalent in the other currency.
Rate displayed: the live interbank rate (mid-market), with a small label "Live interbank rate. Updated [timestamp]."
Optional secondary line: "Most banks add a 2–4% markup to this rate."
Default amount on page load: ₹50,000 → CZK equivalent.
Data source: any reliable FX API. Cache rate for 5–15 minutes.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
Prague has an excellent integrated public transport system — three metro lines, an extensive tram network, and buses. A 24-hour, 72-hour, or longer day-pass gives unlimited travel across the network. Buy at metro stations, tram-stop machines, or through the PID Lítačka mobile app. Validate paper tickets in the yellow validators when boarding — uninspected tickets attract fines.
Prague Airport is connected to the city by Bus 119 to Nadrazi Veleslavin metro station, a private airport-shuttle service, or taxi. Use the official AAA Radiotaxi or Tick Tack Taxi from the airport's taxi counter, or book Bolt or Uber, which are reliable. Ignore the unofficial drivers who approach in arrivals.
Inside the Old Town, walking is faster than any other transport. Cars are pointless. Trams are scenic and useful for the longer journeys to areas like Holesovice, Karlin, and Vinohrady.

schengen.meta_title: Schengen and Eurozone Travel Guide for Indians: Currencies, Visa, Money (2026)
schengen.meta_desc: A practical guide for Indians planning a multi-country European trip. Schengen vs Eurozone, the new EES system, ETIAS, the 90/180 rule, and forex strategy for crossing borders.
schengen.h1: Schengen and Eurozone Travel Guide for Indians: Currencies, Visa, Money (2026)

sections.title: Schengen and Eurozone Are Different Things
sections.body:
Schengen is a borderless travel area. Twenty-nine European countries have agreed to drop passport checks at their internal borders, so a single visa or a single permitted entry covers movement across all of them. The Schengen Area covers 29 countries: Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Croatia, Cyprus (in transitional accession as of mid-2026), Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, Italy, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, Slovenia, Spain, Sweden, and Switzerland. Ireland is in the European Union but is not part of Schengen and runs its own visa regime. The United Kingdom left the EU in 2020 and is not in Schengen either.
The Eurozone is a currency union. Twenty European Union member states have adopted the Euro as their official currency. Bulgaria became the twentieth Eurozone member on 1 January 2026. The Eurozone members as of 2026 are Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Croatia, Cyprus, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Portugal, Slovakia, Slovenia, and Spain.
The two lists overlap heavily but are not the same. Nine Schengen countries do not use the Euro: Czech Republic uses the Czech Koruna, Denmark the Danish Krone, Hungary the Forint, Poland the Złoty, Sweden the Swedish Krona, Iceland the Icelandic Króna, Norway the Norwegian Krone, and Switzerland and Liechtenstein the Swiss Franc. Travelling these countries with Euro cash and expecting to spend it directly fetches a poor exchange rate at the till. A typical Indian trip combining Vienna and Prague, or Switzerland and Italy, or Copenhagen and Amsterdam, crosses the Schengen border without a passport check but crosses the currency border with an exchange rate impact every time.

sections.title: How Much of Each Currency to Carry
sections.body:
Two-tone shading: Eurozone Schengen members in one shade, non-Eurozone Schengen members in another. A small legend at the side maps each non-Euro country to its currency (CHF, CZK, DKK, HUF, PLN, SEK, ISK, NOK).
Hover or tap on any country brings up a small card: country name, currency, May 2026 INR rate range, cash-vs-card tier (one of: Cash-heavy, Mixed, Card-heavy, Cashless).
The clean way to plan multi-country forex is to break the itinerary into currency zones, not country zones. A Switzerland-and-Italy trip is a CHF zone and a EUR zone. A Vienna-Prague-Budapest trip is a EUR zone followed by a CZK zone followed by a HUF zone. Allocate cash and forex card loads by zone, not by country.
A Sensible Multi-Currency Plan
For Eurozone-only trips (France, Italy, Spain, Germany, Netherlands, Greece, and the rest), one currency covers the whole trip. Load Euros on a forex card, carry a modest Euro cash float, and ATM-withdraw more on the ground if needed.
For mixed trips, a multi-currency forex card is the most efficient option. Load Euros plus the secondary currency or currencies for the non-Eurozone leg. The Matrix Forex Card supports 28 currencies and lets multiple loaded currencies sit on the same physical card. Each loaded currency works on a one-to-one basis when spending in that currency, with no cross-currency conversion fee.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip across all Schengen destinations combined. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. A multi-country Schengen trip can come close to the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold once flights, hotels, and on-ground spending are added, particularly if Switzerland or the Nordics are on the route. Above that threshold, the 20 percent TCS applies on the amount above ₹10 lakh, and it is recoverable against your final income tax liability when you file your return.

sections.title: Where to Buy European Currency Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest European currency you will buy is the currency you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Europe, the rates at airport exchange counters and tourist-area money changers are universally poor. The independent kiosks branded with familiar logos at every European airport are the worst-rate channel by some margin. The 0% Commission booths in Prague, the Cambio counters in Italy, the Geldwechsel kiosks in Vienna, and the airport Travelex equivalents at every major hub all run wider spreads than any honest channel.
Major Eurozone bank ATMs (Deutsche Bank, BNP Paribas, ING, Santander) and major non-Eurozone bank ATMs (UBS in Switzerland, ČSOB in Czech Republic, Nordea in Sweden) work reliably with foreign cards at reasonable rates. Avoid the independent Euronet ATMs in tourist areas across all Schengen countries — their rates and fees run worse than bank ATMs.
The cleanest approach: order Euros and any secondary currencies for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and use bank ATMs only for cash top-ups.

sections.title: Cards Across Schengen
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard work at almost every retail terminal across all 29 Schengen countries. American Express has narrower acceptance at small businesses but works at hotels and large retailers. RuPay has effectively no acceptance and should not be the primary card. Apple Pay and Google Pay work everywhere a contactless terminal works, which is most of Western and Northern Europe.
3D Secure verification applies on most online card payments above a small floor across the European Union. This means an OTP sent to your registered Indian mobile number for many bookings — train tickets on national rail websites, museum reservations, hotel bookings on smaller sites. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. A failed OTP at the booking stage often means the booking does not complete and the inventory is released.
At hotels, large department stores, and tourist-area restaurants, the card terminal asks whether to charge in the local currency or in your home currency. Always pick the local currency. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes. The fine print on the terminal sometimes phrases it as a convenience or a courtesy. It is neither.

sections.title: ETIAS and the New Border System
sections.body:
Two changes to the European border system matter for Indian travellers planning trips from late 2026 onward.
The Entry/Exit System (EES) went live on 10 April 2026. EES is the digital replacement for the old passport-stamping system. On first entry into Schengen, the system captures fingerprints and a face photo. Subsequent entries within the validity of the same passport require only a quick scan. Passport stamps are no longer applied at most major Schengen airports and ports. The system tracks every entry and every exit automatically, and the 90-day count runs in the database rather than on paper. Expect 5 to 10 minutes of additional processing on a first entry; subsequent entries move faster than the old stamp system did. Smaller land borders may continue stamping passports as the rollout completes.
ETIAS — the European Travel Information and Authorisation System — launches in the fourth quarter of 2026 and becomes mandatory in 2027. ETIAS is a pre-travel online authorisation similar in concept to the US ESTA, costing a small Euro fee and valid for 3 years or until passport expiry. For Indian passport holders, ETIAS will not initially apply, because Indians already require a Schengen visa, which contains the equivalent vetting. Indians on multi-entry Schengen visas should still understand the system because future visa-policy revisions may interact with it.
The 90/180-Day Rule
A short-stay Schengen visa permits 90 days of stay within any rolling 180-day window across all Schengen countries combined. The rule is calculated cumulatively. Sixty days in France plus thirty days in Germany within the same six-month window equals 90 days used.
Before EES, the count was tracked on paper through passport stamps. Border agents counted stamps, and small misjudgements were often missed. Post-EES, the count is automatic and tightly enforced. Overstays — even by one or two days — are caught at the next exit and lead to entry bans of 6 months to several years. Plan multi-country itineraries with the cumulative count in mind, particularly for travellers making more than one Schengen trip in a single calendar year.
If a trip needs to exceed 90 days within the window, the options are a national long-stay visa (a D-visa from a specific country), splitting the trip across non-Schengen Europe — Ireland, the United Kingdom, and a few Eastern European countries outside Schengen — or scheduling the trip with a long enough gap to clear the 180-day rolling window.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
Daily spending across Schengen varies dramatically by country. The general split runs as follows.
Lower-cost Schengen: Czech Republic, Hungary, Poland, the Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania), Slovakia, and Croatia. A mid-range trip in any of these runs at roughly half the cost of Western Europe.
Mid-cost Schengen: Spain, Portugal, Italy outside Milan, Greece, and most of Germany. Comfortable mid-range trips that do not feel punishing on a typical Indian leisure budget.
Higher-cost Schengen: France (especially Paris), the Netherlands, Belgium, Austria, and most large German cities. Mid-range trips here are noticeably more expensive.
Premium Schengen: Switzerland, Norway, Iceland, Sweden, and Denmark. Daily costs run substantially above the Western European average. Switzerland and Norway are typically the most expensive countries in the world for a leisure trip from India.
A 10-day Schengen itinerary that combines lower-cost Eastern European cities with one or two days in a Western capital sits at a comfortable mid-range. The same itinerary swapping the Eastern leg for Switzerland or Norway runs noticeably higher.
Two-input live converter: enter an amount in INR or EUR, see the equivalent in the other currency.
Rate displayed: the live interbank rate (mid-market), with a small label "Live interbank rate. Updated [timestamp]."
Optional secondary line: "Most banks add a 2–4% markup to this rate."
Default amount on page load: ₹50,000 → EUR equivalent.
Note for this page: a small footer note that EUR is the primary currency for 20 of 29 Schengen countries; for trips covering CHF, CZK, SEK, DKK, NOK, HUF, PLN, or ISK, see the destination-specific guides for those rate ranges.
Data source: any reliable FX API. Cache rate for 5–15 minutes.

sections.title: Applying for a Schengen Visa from India
sections.body:
Indian passport holders need a Schengen visa for any Schengen country. The visa is a single sticker in the passport that covers travel across all 29 Schengen members within its validity. The standard short-stay tourist visa fee is around 90 Euros for adults, with reduced fees for children. VFS Global service charges, biometrics, and mandatory Schengen-compliant travel insurance add to the total. The end-to-end cost typically lands in the ₹12,000 to ₹15,000 range per applicant, depending on the country of application.
The application rule is to apply at the embassy of the country where you will spend the most days. If days are equal across two countries, apply at the country of first entry. Applying at the wrong country is a common reason for outright refusal. France, Germany, Italy, Spain, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and Greece all run separate VFS centres in major Indian cities.
Processing typically takes 3 to 4 weeks at standard service. Peak season (May to July, October to December) extends to 6 weeks or longer. Apply at least 2 to 3 months before flying during peak season. The legal minimum lead time is 15 days but the practical reality is much longer.
Documentation requirements include flight bookings (refundable bookings recommended), hotel bookings for the entire trip, Schengen-compliant travel insurance with adequate coverage, bank statements for the last 3 to 6 months, employment letters or business registration documents, and biometric photos. The application also requires a clear itinerary that justifies the days requested.
Common rejection reasons include weak ties to India (no salary slips, no property documents), incomplete or implausible itineraries, and insufficient bank balance. A rejection is not a permanent ban — applicants may reapply with corrected documentation. The cascade visa system rewards repeat travellers: first-time Indian applicants typically receive a single-entry visa matching the trip dates, second-time applicants often receive a 2-year multi-entry, and frequent travellers move to 5-year multi-entry visas over time.

sections.title: Combining Schengen with Switzerland, the UK, or Ireland
sections.body:
Switzerland is in Schengen but is not in the European Union and is not in the Eurozone. A standard Schengen visa covers Swiss entry alongside the rest of the Schengen Area. A trip combining Italy and Switzerland, or France and Switzerland, requires only one Schengen visa. The currency changes at the border — Swiss Francs in Switzerland, Euros in the Eurozone neighbours — but the visa does not.
Ireland is in the European Union and uses the Euro, but is not in the Schengen Area. A separate Irish visit visa is required for Indian passport holders. A trip combining Dublin with Paris or Amsterdam needs both a Schengen visa and an Irish visa, applied for through separate processes.
The United Kingdom is not in the European Union and not in Schengen. A separate UK Standard Visitor visa is required. A combined London and Paris trip needs both a Schengen visa and a UK visa.
This trio of separate-visa countries — UK, Ireland, Switzerland — is also the standard way to extend a long European trip beyond the 90-day Schengen limit, since time spent in the UK or Ireland does not count against the Schengen 90/180 limit.
A short interactive checklist (10 items) the reader can tick off pre-departure. State persists in browser localStorage.
Items:
1. Schengen visa stamped in passport, applied at the country of most days
2. Passport with at least 6 months validity and 2 blank pages
3. Forex card with EUR loaded as primary spend method, plus secondary currencies for non-Eurozone Schengen countries on the route
4. Modest cash float in EUR for arrival, plus small floats in any non-Eurozone currencies on the route (CHF, CZK, etc.)
5. Visa or Mastercard contactless credit card with low foreign exchange fees as backup
6. Indian SIM active on roaming or eSIM with Indian number reachable for OTP verification
7. Schengen-compliant travel insurance covering minimum €30,000 medical, valid for the entire stay
8. Printed copies of hotel bookings, flight bookings, and visa stamps for border officials at first entry (EES first-time enrolment can ask)
9. Day-by-day itinerary with country boundaries marked, for the 90/180 cumulative count
10. National rail or transport passes pre-booked for any rail-heavy legs (Eurail, Swiss Travel Pass, etc.)

singapore.meta_title: Singapore Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
singapore.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Singapore trip from India. SGD, cards, EZ-Link, transport, costs, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
singapore.h1: Singapore Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Singapore Dollar and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Singapore dollar trades comfortably above the Indian rupee, and the rate is mentally easy — multiply SGD by roughly 60 to 70 to get a rough INR equivalent, and the running mental maths takes care of itself within a day. The currency has been broadly stable to slightly stronger against the rupee through 2025 and into 2026, which is normal for SGD given the Monetary Authority of Singapore's exchange-rate-targeting framework.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.
Singapore is expensive. Hotels, dining, and alcohol are markedly higher than Bangkok or Saigon. The exchange rate is not the part that hurts. The base prices are.

sections.title: How Much SGD to Carry
sections.body:
Singapore is among the most cashless economies in the world. Cards work at almost every retail outlet, restaurant, taxi, and tourist site. Contactless tap-to-pay is universal. PayNow and other QR systems are everywhere on signs but tied to local bank accounts and not directly usable by Indian travellers.
Cash matters mainly at hawker centres, where Visa or Mastercard contactless is increasingly accepted but not universal yet, plus the older wet markets, some traditional shops in Chinatown and Little India, and small mom-and-pop establishments.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of SGD in cash from India for arrival incidentals — a hawker centre meal on day one, a top-up on the EZ-Link card, a taxi from Changi if you skip the MRT. Withdraw additional cash only if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with SGD handles 95 percent of spending.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Singapore. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy SGD Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest SGD you will buy is the SGD you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Singapore, licensed money changers in The Arcade at Raffles Place, People's Park Complex in Chinatown, and Mustafa Centre in Little India offer rates close to the interbank rate, often better than banks and far better than airport counters. They are one of the small genuinely cheaper-than-home things in Singapore. If you find yourself short on cash mid-trip, those are the places to top up.
Skip the airport exchange counters at Changi for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are reasonable but the rates are middling.

sections.title: Cards in Singapore
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted almost universally in Singapore. American Express works at most international hotels, restaurants, and large stores. JCB has decent acceptance through Japanese tourism volumes. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay all work widely. NETS and PayNow are the local alternatives but they are tied to Singapore bank accounts and not directly usable by visitors. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Singapore trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly attractions like Universal Studios Singapore and the Night Safari, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores like Takashimaya and ION, and duty-free counters at Changi, the card machine asks whether to charge in SGD or in Indian rupees. Always pick SGD. The rupee option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Singapore
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere — DBS, OCBC, UOB, Citibank, HSBC, and Standard Chartered all have wide networks. Foreign cards work reliably at almost all of them. Withdrawal fees are moderate. The main consideration is your home bank's foreign-ATM fee, which adds to the cost of every withdrawal.
The cleanest approach is to load a forex card before flying, withdraw a moderate amount in cash on day one, and avoid frequent small withdrawals.
EZ-Link or SimplyGo: The Transit Card
The EZ-Link card is Singapore's stored-value transit card. Tap on, tap off, fare deducts automatically. It works on the MRT, all public buses, and increasingly at retail outlets and parking. Buy it at any MRT station with a small refundable deposit and a few dollars of stored value.
There is also a SimplyGo option that uses your contactless Visa or Mastercard directly — tap your card or phone at the gate and the fare is charged in SGD. Most Indian forex cards work with SimplyGo, which means many travellers can skip buying a separate transit card altogether. Test it on day one before committing.
If you visit Singapore often, an EZ-Link or NETS FlashPay card is worth keeping for return trips. The balance does not expire.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, hawker centre meals (still some of the best food value in the world for the money), MRT and buses, free attractions like Gardens by the Bay's outdoor sections and most museums on free days. Singapore at this tier is more expensive than Bangkok or Saigon but still doable.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Bugis, Clarke Quay, or near Orchard Road, a mix of hawker meals and mid-range restaurants, paid attractions like Universal Studios, the SEA Aquarium, and the Night Safari, occasional taxis, a few experiences like a Singapore Sling at Raffles or a chilli crab dinner.
Premium: five-star hotels like Marina Bay Sands, the Fullerton, or the Capella, fine-dining restaurants, private guides, paid Sentosa attractions, and the high-end shopping budget on Orchard Road. Singapore at this tier is genuinely expensive, comparable to London or Tokyo.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in Singapore costs noticeably more than the same trip to Vietnam or Sri Lanka, and roughly comparable to a Japan trip outside the cherry blossom premium. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
The MRT is fast, clean, and covers almost everywhere a tourist needs to go. EZ-Link or SimplyGo handles the fare. Buses fill in the rest of the network. Singapore is small — most journeys take under 30 minutes.
Taxis are easy to hail and use meters honestly, but they are expensive compared to the MRT. The Grab app is the default for ride-hailing and works with foreign cards. Standard taxis from companies like ComfortDelGro can also be booked through the Grab app.
Walking between the major neighbourhoods is feasible but Singapore's heat and humidity make long walks unpleasant. Plan around indoor connections — much of central Singapore is connected by air-conditioned underground walkways and shopping centres.

south-korea.meta_title: South Korea Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
south-korea.meta_desc: A practical guide to a South Korea trip from India. Won, cards, T-money, transport, etiquette, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
south-korea.h1: South Korea Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Won and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Korean won runs in big numbers, but easier to handle than the Vietnamese dong. A coffee runs around 5,000 won. A subway ride around 1,500. A mid-range hotel room a couple of hundred thousand. Most travellers fall into a comfortable mental rhythm by day two.
The won has been broadly stable to slightly weak against the rupee through 2025 and into 2026. For Indian travellers this means Korea remains accessible, particularly outside the central Seoul tourist districts where prices are still reasonable for a first-world destination.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Won to Carry
sections.body:
South Korea is among the most cashless economies in Asia. Cards work at almost every retail outlet, restaurant, café, convenience store, taxi, and tourist site in Seoul, Busan, Jeju, and the major cities. Contactless and mobile payments are standard. Cash matters at older traditional markets like Gwangjang or Namdaemun, a handful of small family-run restaurants, some street food vendors, and rural areas. Even temple entry fees and parking machines mostly accept cards now.
A modest stock of cash handles the few places that still need it. Most travellers withdraw a small amount on arrival and barely use it through the trip.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of won in cash from India for arrival incidentals — taxi from the airport if you skip the AREX train, an early meal, a bottle of water. Withdraw additional cash at the airport ATM only if needed; rates are reasonable, fees moderate. For most travellers, loading the bulk of the trip budget on a forex card is the cleanest approach. Cards handle 95 percent of spending.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Korea. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Won Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest won you will buy is the won you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Korea, the licensed money changers in Myeongdong and Itaewon often offer better rates than banks for cash exchange. Major banks like KEB Hana and Shinhan are reliable and only slightly worse. Avoid airport exchange counters beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach: order a moderate amount of won for door delivery before flying, load the bulk of your trip budget on a forex card, and use the card for everything except the few cash-only situations.

sections.title: Cards in South Korea
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted almost universally. American Express works at most international hotels and large stores, less reliably at small shops. JCB has decent acceptance through historic Japanese trade links. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay launched in Korea in 2023 and works at most modern terminals. Google Pay has limited acceptance. Samsung Pay dominates the local mobile-payment scene but does not accept foreign-issued cards for top-up. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Korea trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly KTX rail tickets and domestic flights, fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores like Lotte and Shinsegae, and duty-free counters, the card machine asks whether to charge in won or in your home currency. Always pick won. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in South Korea
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere, but not all of them accept foreign cards. The ones marked Global ATM with the Visa, Mastercard, and Plus logos do, and these are the ones to use. They are common in subway stations, convenience store chains like CU, GS25, and 7-Eleven, and at most banks.
Withdrawal fees are moderate — typically a flat fee per withdrawal regardless of amount. Withdrawal limits are generous, so plan a smaller number of larger withdrawals if you need cash.
Skip the airport exchange counters for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival. The kiosks in Myeongdong and Itaewon offer better rates if you need to exchange more.
The T-money Card: Buy It On Day One
T-money is the IC card that runs Korean public transport. Buses, subways, taxis, intercity trains for short hops, and even most convenience stores and vending machines accept T-money. It is the single most useful purchase an Indian traveller can make on day one.
Buy a T-money card at any convenience store at Incheon Airport or any subway station. Top up with cash or card. The basic card costs a few thousand won and the balance never expires. Keep the card for future trips.
There is also a Climate Card and a discount card aimed at tourists, both of which offer flat-fee unlimited transit for a few days at a time. Run the numbers on the Visit Seoul site if your itinerary is heavy on subway and bus travel inside the city.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, two convenience-store or fast-food meals, one cheap restaurant dinner, subway and bus, free palaces and parks. Korea is cheaper than Japan at this tier and the food quality is excellent for the money.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Myeongdong, Hongdae, or Gangnam, two restaurant meals a day, occasional taxis, paid attractions and a few experiences like a Korean barbecue dinner or a jjimjilbang (Korean bathhouse) visit.
Premium: five-star hotel like the Lotte or the Shilla, fine-dining Korean and international restaurants, private guides, a high-end shopping budget for K-beauty and fashion. Comfortable rather than extravagant by Indian premium standards.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two lands well below what the same trip in Japan or Singapore would cost. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.
