sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
The Seoul subway is one of the best urban transit systems in the world. Clean, on time, signposted in English, and cheap. A T-money card handles fares automatically. The KTX high-speed train links Seoul to Busan, Daegu, and Gwangju in two to three hours.
For inter-city journeys, KTX is the default. Express buses are cheaper and serve more towns but take longer. Domestic flights to Jeju are routinely cheaper than the bus or train if booked in advance — Jeju is the busiest air route in the world by passenger volume, and competition keeps fares low.
Inside cities, taxis are cheap by international standards and almost always honest. Use the Kakao T app to book — it works for foreign-issued cards and avoids any taxi-meter awkwardness. Uber technically operates in Seoul but Kakao T is more reliable.

sri-lanka.meta_title: Sri Lanka Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
sri-lanka.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Sri Lanka trip from India. LKR, USD, cards, transport, costs, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
sri-lanka.h1: Sri Lanka Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Sri Lankan Rupee and the Indian Rupee
sections.body:
Both countries call their currency the rupee, which causes occasional confusion. The Sri Lankan rupee (LKR) is a distinct currency and the exchange rate to the Indian rupee is roughly 4 to 4.5 LKR per INR through 2025 and into 2026 — the LKR is the weaker of the two. Mental maths is easy: divide LKR amounts by roughly 4 to get a rough INR equivalent.
The LKR went through a sharp devaluation during Sri Lanka's 2022 economic crisis and has stabilised since 2023. For Indian travellers this stabilisation has been good — Sri Lanka is now noticeably better value than it was before the crisis, and the country is open and welcoming again.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Cash to Carry
sections.body:
Sri Lanka is mainly a cash economy outside the four-star and five-star tourist circles. Tuk-tuks, small restaurants, markets, train tickets, beach shacks, and most local experiences run on cash. Cards work at international hotels in Colombo, Galle, and Kandy, branded restaurants, large supermarkets like Cargills Food City and Keells, big shopping malls, and most upmarket tour operators.
USD is widely accepted in Sri Lanka, particularly at hotels, dive shops, and tour operators in tourist towns. Many businesses prefer USD for larger payments. Both rupees and dollars work — the practical advice is to carry both.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry US dollars from India in clean, undamaged notes — torn, marked, or pre-2009 series notes are sometimes refused at Sri Lankan exchange counters. Convert in batches on arrival rather than all at once. Keep a forex card loaded with USD as backup. Some Indian travellers also choose to carry Indian rupees for emergency cash exchange at the border or in tourist areas, where INR is sometimes accepted at small premiums.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Sri Lanka. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Exchange Money
sections.body:
The cheapest forex you will buy is the USD you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Sri Lanka, the licensed money changers in Colombo's Pettah district and around the major hotels in Galle Face offer the best rates. Major banks like Commercial Bank, Sampath Bank, and HNB are reliable and only slightly worse. Avoid the airport exchange counters at Bandaranaike International for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach for an Indian traveller: order USD for door delivery before flying, carry the bulk in cash, load a moderate amount on a forex card as backup, and exchange in batches at licensed money changers or banks once on the ground.

sections.title: Cards in Sri Lanka
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at international hotels, branded restaurants, large supermarkets, big shopping malls, and most established tour operators in Colombo, Galle, Kandy, and the major beach towns. Acceptance drops sharply outside those circles. American Express works at international hotel chains but not much else. JCB has limited acceptance. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most card terminals that accept contactless, though contactless penetration is lower in Sri Lanka than in India. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Sri Lanka often trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly for trains and domestic flights, fail at the OTP step.
At hotels and large restaurants, the card machine sometimes asks whether to charge in LKR or in your home currency. Always pick LKR. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Sri Lanka
sections.body:
ATMs are common in Colombo and tourist towns. Commercial Bank, Sampath Bank, HNB, and BOC have wide networks. Foreign cards work reliably at most of them. Withdrawal fees are moderate. Withdrawal limits per transaction tend to be moderate compared to Indian standards — typically around LKR 60,000 to 100,000 per withdrawal.
Skip the airport ATMs at Bandaranaike for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are high and the rates are poor. Walk to the licensed money changers in Pettah or use the ATM at your hotel for better economics.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: a guesthouse room, three rice-and-curry meals at small restaurants, train and bus, free beaches and temples. Sri Lanka at this tier is one of the cheapest serious-travel destinations in South Asia.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel or a heritage boutique property, two restaurant meals a day, a private driver for day trips between Colombo, Kandy, and the Hill Country, paid attractions like Sigiriya, Polonnaruwa, and the Temple of the Tooth, occasional spa visits and Ayurveda treatments.
Premium: five-star resorts in Galle, Yala, or the Hill Country, fine-dining restaurants, private guides, helicopter tours, and the high-end safari and wellness budget. Sri Lanka at this tier offers strong value for the money compared to comparable resort destinations.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in Sri Lanka, including a private driver, lands well below what the same trip in Singapore or Japan would cost. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
A private driver-cum-guide is the default for Indian travellers doing the classic Sri Lanka loop — Colombo, Sigiriya, Kandy, Nuwara Eliya, Ella, Galle, Yala. Rates are reasonable and most hotels can arrange one. The driver doubles as a guide for shorter day trips.
The train from Kandy to Ella is one of the most photographed train rides in Asia for a reason. Book second-class reserved or observation-car tickets a few weeks in advance through the official Sri Lanka Railways site or a trusted booking agent. Tickets sell out for the famous segments.
Inside the cities, tuk-tuks are the default. Apps like PickMe and Uber operate in Colombo and Kandy and avoid the bargaining headache of street tuk-tuks. Outside the apps, agree the fare before getting in. A meter is rare; an honest quote is normal.

switzerland.meta_title: Switzerland Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Swiss Franc and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Swiss franc is one of the strongest currencies in the world. It trades comfortably above both the Euro and the rupee, and it has been broadly stable to slightly stronger against the rupee through 2025 and into 2026. Mental maths is cleaner than it sounds: multiply CHF by roughly 100 to get a rough INR equivalent, and the running maths takes care of itself.
Switzerland is expensive in a way that surprises first-time visitors. A coffee in Zurich runs more than a sit-down meal in many parts of India. A simple lunch lands well above what a similar meal would cost in Paris or Rome. The exchange rate is not the part that hurts. The base prices are. Plan accordingly.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Swiss Franc to Carry
sections.body:
Switzerland is among the most card-friendly countries in Europe. Cards work at almost every retail outlet, restaurant, hotel, train ticket machine, and tourist site. Contactless payments are universal at modern terminals. Cash matters at small mountain restaurants and refuges, public toilets in train stations (which usually take coins), some farmers' markets, and remote rural areas where the village shop has not modernised.
A modest stock of CHF in cash handles every situation that still needs it. Most travellers withdraw or carry a small amount and barely use it through the trip.
One small note for Indian travellers: Euros are accepted at many Swiss tourist-area shops and hotels but at distinctly poor rates. The change comes back in CHF at a rate the merchant chooses. Always pay in CHF if you can — it is the cleaner outcome.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of CHF in cash from India for arrival incidentals — train ticket from the airport, a coffee, a public toilet at a station. Withdraw additional cash on the ground only if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with CHF handles 95 percent of spending.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Switzerland. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. A typical Switzerland trip may approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold once flights, hotels, and on-ground spending are added together — if your total LRS spending in the financial year crosses that threshold, the 20 percent TCS applies on the amount above ₹10 lakh, and it is recoverable against your final income tax liability when you file your return.

sections.title: Where to Buy Swiss Francs Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest Swiss francs you will buy are the francs you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Switzerland, the rates at airport exchange counters and tourist-area money changers are mediocre. Major banks like UBS, Credit Suisse's successor entities, and Raiffeisen offer better rates, but the spread is narrow and not worth a special trip if you have already loaded a forex card. Skip airport exchange counters at Zurich, Geneva, and Basel for anything beyond emergency cash.
The cleanest approach for an Indian traveller: order a moderate amount of CHF for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and rely on the card for almost all spending.

sections.title: Cards in Switzerland
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted almost universally in Switzerland — at hotels, restaurants, train ticket machines, mountain railways, supermarkets, and shops. American Express works at most international hotels and large restaurants, less reliably at small establishments. JCB has reasonable acceptance through Japanese tourism volumes. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay all work widely. TWINT, the Swiss QR payment system, is everywhere on signs but tied to local Swiss bank accounts and not directly usable by visitors. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Switzerland trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly the SBB Mobile rail app and individual mountain railway tickets, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores, and watch shops, the card machine asks whether to charge in CHF or in your home currency. Always pick CHF. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes — and on Swiss prices, that markup can be a meaningful sum.

sections.title: ATMs in Switzerland
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere — UBS, PostFinance, and Raiffeisen all have wide networks. Foreign cards work reliably at most of them. Withdrawal fees are moderate. Withdrawal limits are generous. The main consideration is your home bank's foreign-ATM fee, which adds to the cost of every withdrawal.
The cleanest approach is to load a forex card before flying, withdraw a moderate amount on day one if cash is needed, and avoid frequent small withdrawals.
The Swiss Travel Pass: The Single Biggest Trip Decision
Switzerland's public transport network is exceptional — trains, buses, lake boats, and many mountain railways form a single integrated system. The Swiss Travel Pass is a flat-fee unlimited-use ticket across most of that system. For the kind of itinerary most Indian travellers plan — Zurich, Lucerne, Interlaken, Jungfrau region, Zermatt, Lake Geneva — the pass usually pays for itself in 4 to 7 days.
The pass also covers free admission to most museums, free travel on lake boats, and substantial discounts on the famous mountain excursions like the Jungfraujoch, Schilthorn, and Mount Pilatus. The Top of Europe trip alone is so expensive that the discount on it covers a meaningful share of the pass cost.
Buy the pass online before flying or at any major Swiss train station. The 4-day, 6-day, 8-day, and 15-day options cover most itineraries. Children under 16 travelling with a parent on a Swiss Travel Pass travel free on a Swiss Family Card. Run the numbers on the official Swiss Travel System site against your specific itinerary before buying.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in Switzerland splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, supermarket meals from Migros or Coop (genuinely good and affordable), local trains on the Swiss Travel Pass, free outdoor attractions like alpine hiking and lakeside walks. Switzerland at this tier is feasible but still significantly more expensive than most of Europe — there is no cheap Switzerland.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel in Lucerne, Interlaken, or Zermatt, two restaurant meals a day with the breakfast usually included, the Swiss Travel Pass for transport, paid mountain excursions like the Jungfraujoch or Mount Titlis.
Premium: five-star resorts like the Burgenstock or the Mont Cervin Palace in Zermatt, fine-dining experiences, helicopter tours, and the high-end watch and shopping budget on Bahnhofstrasse and Rue du Rhône. Switzerland at this tier rivals any premium destination in the world.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two is one of the more expensive holidays you can plan from India, comparable to Japan in cherry blossom season but typically higher. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
Trains run Switzerland. The SBB network is fast, on time, and integrated with buses, lake boats, and mountain railways. The Swiss Travel Pass or a point-to-point ticket combined with a Half Fare Card covers most situations. The SBB Mobile app handles bookings and shows live timetables and platforms.
Driving is feasible but not necessary. Swiss roads are excellent and the alpine driving is genuinely scenic, but parking in cities is expensive and the train network is so good that most Indian itineraries do not benefit from a car. The exception is if you are basing yourself in a single chalet town and using it as a base for the surrounding region.
From the airports, the train is almost always the right answer. Zurich Airport has its own SBB station underneath Terminal 1. Geneva Airport is similarly direct. Skip the airport taxis unless you have specific accessibility needs.

vienna.meta_title: Vienna Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
vienna.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Vienna trip from India. Euro, cards, transport, costs, opera tickets, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
vienna.h1: Vienna Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Euro and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Euro trades at a meaningful premium to the Indian rupee, with the running rate landing in the 90 to 100 INR per Euro range through 2025 and into 2026. The Euro has been broadly stable to slightly stronger against the rupee in the period. Vienna prices are at the European mainstream — noticeably higher than Prague, comparable to Berlin or Madrid, lower than Zurich or London.
Mental maths is reasonably easy: multiply Euros by roughly 100 to get a rough INR equivalent. The running maths takes care of itself within a day.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a few lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Euro to Carry
sections.body:
Vienna is broadly card-friendly. Cards work at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains like Billa and Spar, museums, and tourist sites. Contactless tap-to-pay is widely supported. Cash matters at traditional Beisl-style Viennese restaurants, some smaller cafes, the Naschmarkt vendors, the Christmas markets, and a few older establishments where the owner prefers cash.
Austria has a slightly higher cash preference than Germany or France in some niche categories, but for most tourist activities cards are the norm.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry a small amount of Euros in cash from India for arrival incidentals — the airport train into the city, a coffee, a pretzel, an early meal. Withdraw additional cash on the ground only if needed. For most travellers, a forex card loaded with Euros handles 90 percent of spending and a moderate cash cushion handles the rest.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Austria. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Buy Euros Before You Fly
sections.body:
The cheapest Euros you will buy are the Euros you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Vienna, the rates at airport exchange counters and tourist-area money changers are mediocre. Major banks like Erste Bank, Bank Austria, and Raiffeisen offer better rates with low spread, but with cards working almost everywhere the need for cash exchange is limited. Skip airport exchange counters at Vienna International for anything beyond the immediate cash you need on arrival.
The cleanest approach: order Euros for door delivery before flying, load the bulk on a forex card, and rely on the card for almost all spending.

sections.title: Cards in Vienna
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at most hotels, restaurants, cafes, supermarket chains, museums, and tourist sites in Vienna. American Express works at international hotels and large stores, less reliably at smaller restaurants and cafes. JCB has limited acceptance. RuPay has effectively no acceptance, so do not rely on it as your main card.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most modern card terminals. A standard Visa-based forex card works wherever Visa is accepted.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Austria trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly OBB train tickets and Vienna State Opera tickets, sometimes fail at the OTP step.
At hotels, large department stores, and tourist-area restaurants, the card machine asks whether to charge in Euros or in your home currency. Always pick Euros. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Vienna
sections.body:
Bank-branded ATMs from Erste Bank, Bank Austria, BAWAG, and Raiffeisen are everywhere and offer reasonable fees and rates. Avoid the independent Euronet ATMs that dominate tourist areas in some other European cities — they appear in Vienna too in tourist zones, and their rates and fees are dramatically worse than bank ATMs.
Withdrawal limits at Vienna ATMs are generous and fees are moderate. Skip the airport ATMs for anything beyond emergency cash.

sections.title: The Vienna Public Transport Pass
sections.body:
Vienna's public transport — five U-Bahn lines, an extensive tram network, S-Bahn trains, and buses — is run by Wiener Linien on a single integrated ticket system. The 24-hour, 48-hour, 72-hour, and 8-day climate tickets give unlimited rides across the network. The Vienna City Card adds discounts on attractions on top.
Buy tickets at the WienMobil app, ticket machines at every major station, or staffed counters. There are no entry gates at most stations — you are expected to validate the ticket on first use and have a valid ticket at all times. Inspectors check randomly and fines are large for travelling without a valid ticket.
From Vienna International Airport (Schwechat), the City Airport Train (CAT) is the fastest option to the centre but the priciest. The S7 S-Bahn is much cheaper and only slightly slower. Either is preferable to the airport taxi for most travellers.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending in Vienna splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: hostel dorm bed, supermarket and bakery meals, public transport on a city pass, free attractions like the Innere Stadt walk, the outdoor parts of Schönbrunn Palace, and the museum free-day rotations. Vienna at this tier is feasible and affordable for an EU capital.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-or-four-star hotel near the Innere Stadt or Mariahilf, two restaurant meals a day with a mix of traditional Viennese and modern Austrian cuisine, public transport, paid attractions like the Schönbrunn and Belvedere palaces, the Albertina and Kunsthistorisches museums, and a coffee-house breakfast at one of the famous Kaffeehäuser.
Premium: five-star hotels like the Sacher or the Imperial, fine dining at restaurants like Steirereck or the Anna Sacher, private guides, an opera or Vienna Philharmonic concert in proper seats, the high-end shopping budget on Kohlmarkt and Tuchlauben.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two in Vienna costs more than the same trip in Prague but less than Paris or Zurich. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

vietnam.meta_title: Vietnam Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)
vietnam.meta_desc: A practical guide to a Vietnam trip from India. Dong, USD, cards, cash, transport, scams, and the things Indian travellers often get wrong.
vietnam.h1: Vietnam Travel Guide for Indians: Money, Cards & Practical Tips (2026)

sections.title: The Dong, the Dollar, and the Rupee
sections.body:
The Vietnamese dong runs in big numbers. A bowl of pho costs around 50,000 dong. A mid-range hotel night runs into the millions. The trick most travellers miss on day one is dropping the last three zeros in your head — divide by a thousand and the number becomes manageable.
Vietnam has a soft dual-currency culture. Hotels, tour operators, dive shops, and high-end restaurants will quote in US dollars. Street food, taxis, markets, and most shops quote in dong. You will spend in both. USD is widely accepted in Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh City, Da Nang, and the major resort towns, though paying in dong almost always gets a better effective rate.
Rates move every day. The number on Google is the live interbank rate, what authorised dealers use to trade with each other. It is not the rate most banks and forex providers actually sell at. They build a markup into their selling price, usually 2 to 4 percent, and it never appears as a separate line item on the bill. On a forex order of a couple of lakh rupees, that markup quietly adds up to thousands.

sections.title: How Much Cash to Carry
sections.body:
Vietnam is a cash economy for everyday spending. Street food, taxis, markets, family-run guest houses, intercity buses, and most domestic experiences run on cash. Cards work at four-star and five-star hotels, large restaurants in Hanoi and Saigon, supermarket chains, and big tourist sites. Outside that, plan to pay cash.
There is one important constraint Indian travellers should know. Vietnamese law restricts the export of large amounts of dong out of the country. The practical rule for a tourist is to convert the bulk of your forex on arrival in Vietnam, spend it, and avoid leaving with a wallet full of dong. Carry small leftover amounts back if you must, but do not stockpile dong in India before flying. The cleanest approach is to carry US dollars from India and convert on the ground, or load a forex card with USD.
A Sensible Cash Plan
Carry US dollars from India in clean, undamaged notes — torn, marked, or pre-2009 series notes are routinely refused at Vietnamese exchange counters. Convert in batches on arrival rather than all at once. Keep a forex card loaded with USD as backup, both for ATM withdrawals in Vietnam and for hotel and restaurant payments where cards are accepted.
Indian residents can carry up to USD 3,000 equivalent in foreign currency notes per trip to Vietnam. Anything beyond that goes on a forex card or as a wire transfer. Most leisure trips will not approach the ₹10 lakh per person per financial year TCS threshold, so no tax collection at source applies.

sections.title: Where to Exchange Money
sections.body:
The cheapest forex you will buy is the USD you buy in India before flying. Indian airport counters and tourist-area money changers run a noticeable markup over the interbank rate. Hotel front desks run worse. Online forex providers selling at the live interbank rate are typically a few percent better than both.
Inside Vietnam, gold shops in Hanoi's Old Quarter and the Ben Thanh Market area in Saigon historically offer the best USD-to-dong rates, often better than banks. Major banks like Vietcombank, BIDV, and Sacombank are reliable and only slightly worse. Avoid airport exchange counters and hotel front desks — they run several percentage points off the mid-market rate.
The cleanest approach for an Indian traveller: order USD for door delivery before flying, carry the bulk in cash, load some on a forex card as backup, and exchange in batches at gold shops or banks once on the ground.

sections.title: Cards in Vietnam
sections.body:
Visa and Mastercard are accepted at four-star and five-star hotels, large restaurants, supermarket chains, big-name shops, and most established tour operators in Hanoi, Saigon, Da Nang, and Hoi An. Acceptance drops sharply outside those circles. American Express works at international hotel chains but not much else. JCB has decent acceptance through Japanese investment in the country. RuPay has effectively no acceptance.
Apple Pay and Google Pay work at most card terminals that accept contactless. Local QR wallets like MoMo, ZaloPay, and VNPay are everywhere on signs but do not accept foreign-issued cards for top-up. Indian travellers cannot use them.

sections.title: Two Things Indian Cardholders Should Know
sections.body:
Online payments in Vietnam often trigger 3D Secure verification, which means an OTP to your registered Indian mobile number. Keep your Indian SIM active on roaming, or use an eSIM that keeps the number reachable. Otherwise online bookings, particularly for trains and domestic flights, fail at the OTP step.
At hotels and large restaurants, the card machine sometimes asks whether to charge in dong or in your home currency. Always pick dong. The home-currency option is dynamic currency conversion and adds a markup of several percent on top of whatever your card already takes.

sections.title: ATMs in Vietnam
sections.body:
ATMs are everywhere in Hanoi, Saigon, Da Nang, and most tourist towns. The trick is choosing the right one. Foreign-card-friendly ATMs from HSBC, ANZ, Citibank, and Vietcombank tend to charge moderate fees and dispense reliably. Local-only ATMs sometimes refuse foreign cards or charge punitive fees.
Withdrawal limits per transaction tend to be lower than what Indian travellers are used to — often capped at 2 to 5 million dong (a relatively small amount in real terms) per withdrawal. Plan multiple withdrawals if you need cash for a multi-day trip outside the cities.
Skip the airport ATMs in Hanoi and Saigon for anything beyond emergency cash. The fees are high and the rates are poor. Walk into the city, find a Vietcombank or HSBC branch, withdraw there.

sections.title: What You Will Spend Each Day
sections.body:
On the ground, daily spending splits into three rough tiers.
Backpacker: dorm bed in a hostel, two pho or banh mi meals, one cheap restaurant dinner, local buses or grab bikes. Vietnam is one of the cheapest serious-travel destinations in Asia at this tier.
Mid-range, where most Indian travellers land: a three-star hotel in the city centre, two restaurant meals a day, a private car for day trips, paid attractions and museums, a mid-range cooking class or boat tour every couple of days.
Premium: four-or-five-star hotel, fine-dining restaurants, private guides, helicopter or seaplane transfers, the high-end resort wings of Phu Quoc and Da Nang. Comfortable rather than extravagant by Indian premium standards.
A 7-day mid-range trip for two is one of the better-value Southeast Asian holidays you can plan. Flights and hotels are usually paid in India before departure and sit on top.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.body:
Domestic flights, sleeper trains, and tourist buses cover the long distances. The country is over 1,600 kilometres top to bottom, and trying to do it all by road is exhausting. Most itineraries fly between Hanoi, Da Nang, and Saigon, and use ground transport for shorter hops.
Inside the cities, Grab is the default for taxis and bike-taxis. Download it before flying. Pay through the app or in cash. Avoid hailing taxis off the street unless they are from established companies like Mai Linh or Vinasun — meter scams are common with independents.
Renting a motorbike is tempting and it is the genuine Vietnam experience for some travellers. It is also where most foreign tourist injuries happen. Indian licences are not technically valid for motorbikes in Vietnam, and rental scams over scratches and damage are common. Skip it unless you are an experienced rider and have read the trip reports.
